title: Vector Properties: Length and Names
description: Learn about vector length, naming elements, and the structure of vectors.

slides.title: Length of a Vector 📏
slides.content:
Every vector has a length, which you can get with `length()`.

```r
x <- c(10, 20, 30)
length(x)  # 3

y <- 5      # single number is a vector of length 1
length(y)  # 1

z <- c()    # empty vector
length(z)  # 0
```

slides.title: Check Length
slides.codeSnippet:
v1 <- 1:10
v2 <- seq(0,1,length.out=5)
v3 <- rep('a',3)

print(length(v1))
print(length(v2))
print(length(v3))

quizData.question: What is the length of `rep(1:3, each = 2, times = 3)`?
quizData.explanation: each = 2 makes length 6 (1,1,2,2,3,3), then times = 3 repeats that 3 times, total 18.

slides.title: Naming Vector Elements 🏷️
slides.content:
You can assign names to individual elements of a vector, which makes indexing easier.

Use `names()` to get or set names.

```r
x <- c(1, 2, 3)
names(x) <- c('a', 'b', 'c')
print(x)
# a b c 
# 1 2 3
```

You can also create a named vector directly:

```r
y <- c(first = 10, second = 20, third = 30)
```

slides.title: Naming Examples
slides.codeSnippet:
scores <- c(85, 92, 78)
names(scores) <- c('Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie')
print(scores)

# Access by name
print(scores['Bob'])

# Remove names
names(scores) <- NULL
print(scores)

quizData.question: Given `v <- c(a=1, b=2, c=3)`, how do you access the element named 'b'?
quizData.explanation: You can use `v['b']` or `v[2]` (by position), but `v$b` is for lists, not vectors. The question asks specifically for the name, so `v['b']` is the direct answer.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
You can use `setNames()` to create a named vector in a pipeline:

```r
setNames(c(1,2,3), c('x','y','z'))
```
